name: Probability
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 13.1 Conditional Probability
sections.body:
For events $E,F$ in a sample space with $P(F)\neq 0$, the conditional probability of $E$ given $F$ is

$P(E\mid F)=\dfrac{P(E\cap F)}{P(F)}.$

Properties:

  • $0\leq P(E\mid F)\leq 1$.
  • $P(S\mid F)=P(F\mid F)=1$.
  • $P((E\cup G)\mid F)=P(E\mid F)+P(G\mid F)-P(E\cap G\mid F)$.
  • $P(E'\mid F)=1-P(E\mid F)$.

sections.heading: 13.2 Multiplication Theorem on Probability
sections.body:
From the definition of conditional probability:

$P(E\cap F)=P(F)\,P(E\mid F)=P(E)\,P(F\mid E).$

Multiplication theorem (chain rule). $P(E\cap F\cap G)=P(E)\,P(F\mid E)\,P(G\mid E\cap F).$

Generalises to any finite number of events.

sections.heading: 13.3 Independent Events
sections.body:
$E$ and $F$ are independent if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other:

$P(E\cap F)=P(E)\,P(F).$

Equivalently, $P(E\mid F)=P(E)$ (when $P(F)>0$) and $P(F\mid E)=P(F)$ (when $P(E)>0$).

Three events $E,F,G$ are mutually independent if all pairwise products and the triple product factor: $P(E\cap F)=P(E)P(F)$, $P(F\cap G)=P(F)P(G)$, $P(E\cap G)=P(E)P(G)$, AND $P(E\cap F\cap G)=P(E)P(F)P(G).$

Independent $\neq$ mutually exclusive: mutually exclusive events with positive probability are NEVER independent (their intersection is empty).

sections.heading: 13.4 Total Probability & Bayes' Theorem
sections.body:
Let $E_1,E_2,\dots,E_n$ be a partition of the sample space (mutually exclusive, exhaustive, each $P(E_i)>0$). For any event $A$:

Total Probability: $P(A)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E_i)\,P(A\mid E_i).$

Bayes' Theorem. If $P(A)>0$:

$P(E_i\mid A)=\dfrac{P(E_i)\,P(A\mid E_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E_j)\,P(A\mid E_j)}.$

The $P(E_i)$ are prior probabilities; $P(E_i\mid A)$ are posterior probabilities (updated by observing $A$). $P(A\mid E_i)$ are the likelihoods.

sections.heading: 13.5 Random Variables & Probability Distributions
sections.body:
A random variable (r.v.) $X$ is a real-valued function on the sample space.

The probability distribution of a discrete r.v. lists each value $x_i$ with its probability $p_i=P(X=x_i)$, where $0\leq p_i\leq 1$ and $\sum p_i=1$.

Mean (expectation). $\mu=E(X)=\sum_{i} x_i\,p_i.$

Variance. $\sigma^{2}=\operatorname{Var}(X)=\sum_{i}(x_i-\mu)^{2}p_i=E(X^{2})-[E(X)]^{2},$ where $E(X^{2})=\sum x_i^{2}p_i.$

Standard deviation. $\sigma=\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X)}.$

sections.heading: 13.6 Bernoulli Trials & Binomial Distribution
sections.body:
A Bernoulli trial is an experiment with exactly two outcomes, success ($S$) and failure ($F$), with $P(S)=p$ constant from trial to trial. Bernoulli trials are independent.

If $X$ is the number of successes in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials each with success probability $p$ (and failure $q=1-p$), then $X$ follows a binomial distribution $B(n,p)$:

$P(X=r)=\binom{n}{r} p^{r}\,q^{n-r},\quad r=0,1,2,\dots,n.$

Properties:

  • Mean: $E(X)=np.$
  • Variance: $\operatorname{Var}(X)=npq.$
  • Standard deviation: $\sigma=\sqrt{npq}.$
  • The probabilities $\binom{n}{0}q^{n},\binom{n}{1}pq^{n-1},\dots,\binom{n}{n}p^{n}$ are the successive terms of the expansion $(q+p)^{n}$.

summary: Conditional probability $P(E\mid F)=P(E\cap F)/P(F)$ leads to the multiplication rule $P(E\cap F)=P(E)P(F\mid E)$ and the definition of independence $P(E\cap F)=P(E)P(F)$. The Total Probability Law sums likelihoods over a partition; Bayes' Theorem reverses conditioning to update priors into posteriors. Random variables have probability distributions with $\sum p_i=1$, mean $E(X)=\sum x_i p_i$, and variance $E(X^{2})-[E(X)]^{2}$. Binomial distribution $B(n,p)$ models successes in $n$ Bernoulli trials with $P(X=r)=\binom{n}{r}p^{r}q^{n-r}$, mean $np$, variance $npq$.
pitfalls: Confusing $P(E\mid F)$ with $P(F\mid E)$ — they are generally different.
Treating mutually exclusive events as independent — opposite is true (mutually exclusive with positive probabilities are dependent).
Using Bayes' Theorem without verifying $E_1,\dots,E_n$ form a partition.
Forgetting $\sum p_i=1$ when computing a missing probability in a distribution.
Applying the binomial formula when trials are not independent or $p$ varies.
